name: La Muela – circular by the Turbal de Navamuño - Espacio Natural de la Sierra de Candelario
name_original: La Muela – circular por el Turbal de Navamuño - Espacio Natural de la Sierra de Candelario
region_intro: Hiking around Candelario offers diverse natural landscapes within the Sierra de Candelario Natural Area. The region features high-mountain terrain, including peaks like Peña Negra and the Calvitero, alongside more accessible paths. Water bodies such as the Cuerpo de Hombre river and the Navamuño Reservoir are prominent features, often accompanied by picturesque peat bogs. Trails frequently lead through varied vegetation, including pine forests, oak trees, and birches. Best hiking trails around Candelario The most popular hiking route is Calvitero and Hoya Moros Loop, an 8.7 miles (13.9 km) trail that takes 6 hours 6 minutes to complete. This difficult route provides high-mountain vistas. Another top favourite among local hikers is Peña Negra and Embalse de Navamuño Loop, a difficult 8.1 miles (13.1 km) path. This trail offers challenging terrain and panoramic views from Peña Negra peak. Local hikers also love the La Muela – circular by the Turbal de Navamuño - Espacio Natural de la Sierra de Candelario, a 6.2 miles (10.0 km) trail leading through a picturesque peat bog near the Cuerpo de Hombre river, often completed in about 3 hours 14 minutes. Hiking around Candelario is defined by diverse mountain trails, picturesque water bodies, and varied vegetation including pine forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from challenging high-mountain routes to more accessible paths. The routes in Candelario are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 40 reviews. More than 400 hikers have used komoot to explore Candelario's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Candelario?
A: Candelario offers a wide variety of hiking experiences, with over 30 trails available. These routes cater to different fitness levels, ranging from easy strolls to challenging high-mountain treks.

Q: What do hikers enjoy most about the trails in Candelario?
A: The hiking trails in Candelario are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 40 reviews. Hikers particularly appreciate the diverse natural landscapes, the breathtaking high-mountain views, and the variety of terrain, including picturesque water bodies and varied vegetation.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking in Candelario?
A: Hiking in Candelario takes you through diverse natural landscapes within the Sierra de Candelario Natural Area. You can expect to see high-mountain terrain, including peaks like Peña Negra, alongside serene water bodies such as the Cuerpo de Hombre river and the Navamuño Reservoir. Trails also lead through varied vegetation, including pine forests, oak trees, and birches, with some routes featuring picturesque peat bogs.

Q: Are there easy or family-friendly hiking options in Candelario?
A: Yes, Candelario offers several accessible paths suitable for leisurely strolls and families. While many routes are challenging, there are 5 easy trails available, providing less strenuous options for those seeking a relaxed outdoor experience amidst the beautiful scenery.

Q: What are some challenging hikes for experienced trekkers in Candelario?
A: For experienced trekkers, Candelario offers 9 difficult routes with significant elevation gain. A notable challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990342292/calvitero-and-lagunas-del-trampal-loop-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calvitero and Lagunas del Trampal Loop Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 14 km with nearly 900 meters of ascent, providing demanding terrain and high-mountain vistas.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Candelario?
A: Yes, Candelario features several excellent circular hiking routes. One popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993356698/pena-negra-and-embalse-de-navamuno-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Negra and Embalse de Navamuño Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers challenging terrain and 360-degree panoramic views from Peña Negra peak.

Q: Can I find trails with water features like rivers or reservoirs?
A: Absolutely. The region is enriched by the Cuerpo de Hombre river and the Navamuño Reservoir, which blends seamlessly with the surrounding landscape. Many routes incorporate these water bodies, and you can even discover a beautiful and picturesque peat bog near the Cuerpo de Hombre river, as seen on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993360254/la-muela-circular-by-the-turbal-de-navamuno-espacio-natural-de-la-sierra-de-candelario" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Muela circular route&lt;/a&gt;. Some trails also feature scenic waterfalls.

Q: Are there trails that offer panoramic views or viewpoints?
A: Yes, many trails in Candelario provide stunning panoramic views. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993356698/pena-negra-and-embalse-de-navamuno-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Negra and Embalse de Navamuño Loop&lt;/a&gt; is known for its 360-degree views from Peña Negra peak. The "La Muela" route also offers beautiful views of the Ambrose Valley, and you can find specific viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2712929" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Ambroz Valley and Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is Candelario a dog-friendly hiking destination?
A: While specific regulations can vary by trail and protected area, many natural spaces in Candelario are generally welcoming to dogs, especially on leash. It's always recommended to check local signage or park guidelines for the specific trail you plan to hike to ensure it's dog-friendly.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Candelario?
A: The diverse climate of Candelario means hiking is enjoyable across multiple seasons. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summer can be warm, especially at lower elevations, while winter provides opportunities for snowshoeing or more challenging hikes in the high mountains, though conditions should be checked carefully.

Q: Are there options for parking at trailheads in Candelario?
A: Yes, many popular trailheads around Candelario offer parking facilities. While specific locations can vary, areas like El Travieso and Camping Cinco Castaños are known for providing access points with parking. It's advisable to check the starting point of your chosen route for detailed parking information.

Q: What interesting places or landmarks can I explore near the hiking trails?
A: Beyond the trails, Candelario's surroundings offer several interesting landmarks. You can visit historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4267031" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carlos V Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, explore natural features such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3243264" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Pilones Natural Pools&lt;/a&gt;, or discover various lakes and reservoirs like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5336708" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navamuño Dam&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2653623" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna del Duque&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there places to eat or relax after a hike in Candelario?
A: Yes, after a rewarding hike, you can find places to refuel and relax. Candelario and its nearby areas offer options such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/987118" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Pequeña Estación Bar Restaurant Café&lt;/a&gt;, providing a convenient spot to enjoy a meal or a drink.
tips: Perfect peak from which to see it from the Sierra de Candelario to the Sierra de Francia, passing through Extremadura. Around the peak there is a ferrata route that can be done with the help of a harness.
Beautiful views and about 100 meters from the Cueva del Bocín de Navamuño.

The cave is located at the end of the Navamuño Valley, where a narrowing of the valley is formed, between the slope of La Muela and the moraine of the old glacier that today forms the source of the river Cuerpo de Hombre.
